name: Red Hill Quarry
short_description: Historic quarry site in the Piedmont region offering opportunities to collect feldspar, quartz, and mica specimens from pegmatite outcrops on gently sloping terrain.
description:
Red Hill Quarry is a notable rockhounding destination in Albemarle County, Virginia, situated within the Virginia Piedmont geological province at 834 feet elevation. The site features exposed pegmatite formations that have attracted collectors and geologists for generations. The quarry's gently sloping terrain makes it accessible for rockhounders of various skill levels looking to explore mineral-rich outcrops.

The primary specimens found here include feldspar, quartz, and mica - classic minerals associated with pegmatite intrusions that formed during the crystallization of granitic magma deep underground. The white to pink feldspar crystals are particularly notable, often reaching substantial sizes within the pegmatite matrix. Quartz specimens range from clear to milky white, while mica sheets can be extracted from certain zones.

The location offers a hands-on learning experience about Piedmont geology and pegmatite formation. Collectors should expect moderate effort to extract quality specimens, as the host rock requires careful working. The gentle slope of the site reduces physical strain compared to steeper quarry locations while still providing productive collecting areas.

Access and collecting practices here depend on current land status - verify ownership and permission before visiting. The Virginia Piedmont has a rich history of mineral extraction dating back centuries, and Red Hill maintains that tradition as a destination for both casual and serious collectors.

Bring appropriate tools for specimen extraction, wear sturdy footwear for navigating uneven quarry surfaces, and plan to spend several hours to yield worthwhile finds. The site rewards patience and knowledge of pegmatite mineral occurrence patterns.
history: Red Hill Quarry sits within the Virginia Piedmont, a region formed by the Grenville orogeny (ancient mountain building event) approximately 1.1 billion years ago, which created the granitic and pegmatitic intrusions that characterize this area. The pegmatite at this location cooled slowly from granitic magma, allowing large mineral crystals including feldspar and mica to develop - minerals that have been of economic and scientific interest throughout Virginia's mining history.
tips: Search the exposed pegmatite faces and broken talus for white to pink feldspar crystals, which often show excellent terminations and can reach several inches in size. Use a hammer and chisel carefully to extract specimens without shattering the host rock - strike perpendicular to crystal faces rather than parallel. Look for zones where mica is more abundant (lighter-colored laminated areas) as these often contain both quality mica and associated feldspar. Bring a small hand lens to identify individual crystal boundaries and plan your extraction strategy before striking.
terrain_notes: Elevation of 834 feet with gentle slopes throughout the quarry area. Uneven and potentially unstable quarry surfaces with scattered rock fragments and talus require sturdy footwear. Rocky, exposed outcrops with minimal vegetation. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas typically 100-400 feet depending on access point. Footing is uneven but not extreme - manageable for hikers with basic fitness.
best_season: April - October. Late spring through early autumn provides optimal conditions with moderate temperatures and lower precipitation that keeps access roads passable and quarry faces dry for safe collecting.
written_directions: From North Garden, proceed north on County Road 688 (Main Street) for approximately 1.5 miles. Continue as the road winds toward higher terrain in the Albemarle foothills. Watch for quarry markers or access signs on the east side of the road. Parking is typically available at a small pullout or clearing near the quarry entrance. GPS navigation to 37.961722, -78.5989 will bring you to the general vicinity - look for exposed rock faces and quarry workings.
road_conditions: County road surface, well-maintained but may be narrow in sections. No high-clearance or 4WD required for standard passenger vehicles under normal conditions.
parking_notes: Small unpaved pullout or clearing near quarry entrance with space for 2-4 vehicles. Limited formal parking facilities.
collection_rules: Permission required from property owner before collecting. Personal collection only - no commercial use. Rockhounders must follow all landowner directives regarding access hours, collecting areas, and specimen limits. Stay within designated quarry areas and do not trespass on surrounding private land.
rules:
Permission from property owner required
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection permitted
Follow all landowner access restrictions
Stay within designated quarry boundaries
nearest_services: North Garden (0.5 miles) has limited services; Charlottesville (approximately 12 miles north) offers full amenities including gas, restaurants, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky quarry terrain with loose stone and potentially unstable surfaces. Moderate hiking ability required to navigate the site safely. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy boots with good ankle support essential. Be prepared for scrambling over talus and working in quarry conditions.

faq.question: What minerals can I find rockhounding at Red Hill Quarry near Charlottesville?
faq.answer: Red Hill Quarry is known for feldspar, quartz, and mica specimens found in pegmatite formations. The feldspar, often white to pink, frequently displays excellent crystal terminations and can reach several inches in size. Quartz is typically milky white or clear, while mica sheets can be carefully extracted from certain zones within the pegmatite.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at Red Hill Quarry in Virginia?
faq.answer: Yes, Red Hill Quarry is private property and permission from the landowner is required before collecting. Contact the property owner to inquire about access and collecting privileges. Always verify current permissions and access rules before your visit, as regulations may change.

faq.question: Is Red Hill Quarry beginner-friendly for rockhounding in Albemarle County?
faq.answer: While the gentle terrain makes navigation easier than steeper quarry sites, Red Hill Quarry requires moderate experience and appropriate tools for safe specimen extraction. Beginners should have someone experienced with quarry collecting techniques accompany them. The pegmatite formation is forgiving for learning specimen extraction compared to harder rock types.

faq.question: What tools do I need for rockhounding at Red Hill Quarry?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer, cold chisel, hand lens for crystal identification, sturdy gloves, and a collection bag. A small pry bar helps extract loosened specimens from the pegmatite matrix. Strike perpendicular to crystal faces rather than parallel to avoid shattering your specimens.

faq.question: What is the best time to visit Red Hill Quarry for rockhounding?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best conditions with moderate temperatures and lower precipitation. Late spring and early autumn provide optimal weather for quarry work. Avoid winter months when quarry faces may be wet or icy, making access and collecting unsafe.

faq.question: How far is Red Hill Quarry from Charlottesville and what are nearby services?
faq.answer: Red Hill Quarry is located approximately 12 miles south of Charlottesville near North Garden, Virginia. North Garden offers limited services just 0.5 miles away, while Charlottesville (12 miles north) provides complete amenities including gas, food, lodging, and rockhounding supplies.

meta_description: Collect feldspar, quartz, and mica at Red Hill Quarry near Charlottesville, Virginia. Private pegmatite quarry with moderate terrain and hands-on specimen collecting.
